name: Bow of Eros
description: The Bow of Eros, also known as Cupid's bow in Roman mythology, is a powerful and iconic artifact associated with the Greek god of love, Eros (Cupid). Forged by Hephaestus, the god of the forge, the bow is crafted from the finest gold or sometimes silver, depending on the account. Its arrows, tipped with either gold or lead, possess the extraordinary ability to incite either uncontrollable love or insurmountable aversion in the hearts of those struck. A golden arrow ignites passionate desire and infatuation, while a leaden arrow creates feelings of intense dislike and rejection. Eros uses this bow to manipulate the affections of gods and mortals alike, often with mischievous or even tragic consequences. The bow is a symbol of the capricious nature of love, highlighting its power to both elevate and destroy. Its existence underscores the idea that love is a force beyond human control, a divine influence that can strike anyone at any time. The Bow of Eros is not merely a weapon; it is an instrument of fate, shaping destinies and dictating the course of relationships throughout the world. Its influence permeates countless myths and legends, serving as a constant reminder of the unpredictable and often overwhelming power of love. The bow represents the vulnerability inherent in matters of the heart, and the potential for both immense joy and devastating heartbreak. Its legacy continues to resonate in modern culture, where it remains a potent symbol of romance and desire.
subtitle: Instrument of Love and Desire

keyMyths.title: Eros and Psyche
keyMyths.description: Eros wounds himself with his own arrow and falls in love with Psyche, a mortal woman. Their story explores themes of love, trust, and the challenges of immortal-mortal relationships.

keyMyths.title: Apollo and Daphne
keyMyths.description: Eros shoots Apollo with a leaden arrow to incite aversion to Daphne, while shooting Daphne with a golden arrow to incite love for Apollo. This causes Daphne to flee from Apollo, eventually turning into a laurel tree.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Mentions Eros as a primordial deity.
sources.text: Mentions Eros as a primordial deity.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: Provides detailed accounts of Eros's arrows and their effects.
